summary: Find concrete methods that could fix the diagnosed bottleneck (untrustworthy admission signal) in self-evolving agents

methods.name: Environment-native programmatic evaluator as the admission gate (tau-bench / AppWorld built-in checkers)
methods.one_liner: Don't judge the transcript at all — gate library admission on the benchmark's own DB-state / unit-test evaluator, the exact signal that produced your +9.4pp oracle.
methods.mechanism: tau-bench compares the final database state against an annotated goal state via hash comparison (plus required-info-to-user checks) and fails policy violations even when the customer is 'happy'; AppWorld defines a programmatic suite of state-based unit tests that snapshot DB state before/after execution and check that ALL expected and NO unexpected changes happened (collateral-damage checks). The admission filter calls this function instead of an LLM reading the chat log.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: it IS ground truth, not a proxy; (2) admission: the gate is a deterministic function, impossible to fool with confident closing language; (3) source: cheat entries that fabricate confirmation numbers score 0 and are rejected outright.
methods.evidence: This is the gold signal by construction — it is what defines success on both benchmarks and what your own oracle library used to get +9.4pp. The 'Silent Failure' study shows transcript-only judges top out at AUROC 0.65 (tau2) / 0.54 (AppWorld) precisely because they don't call it.
methods.limitation: Requires a labeled target-state per task, so it only works during training/offline library-building on benchmark tasks that ship ground truth (both tau2 and AppWorld do); it cannot gate experiences distilled from unlabeled production logs.
methods.portable_to_us: Most portable and highest-ROI. Training-free, zero extra model cost. For offline experience-library optimization on tau2/AppWorld you already have the evaluator in-repo — wire it as the admission gate and the fake-success class disappears. This should be your primary baseline.

methods.name: GroundEval — deterministic trace-contract scoring
methods.one_liner: Replace the LLM judge with deterministic checks that verify the agent's recorded tool-call trace satisfies a machine-readable task contract (access policy, temporal bounds, required actions).
methods.mechanism: A framework adapter records what the agent actually fetched / searched / called and their returns. A per-task machine-readable contract encodes access policies, temporal boundaries, required search spaces, and causal links. Scoring verifies the evidence PATH was valid (were required artifacts actually fetched, in-scope, before the as-of time) rather than whether the answer sounds plausible.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: scores observable external actions, not hidden reasoning/prose; (2) admission: deterministic contract satisfaction, not an LLM opinion.
methods.evidence: Direct head-to-head case study: two frontier LLM judges scored a response 0.90 and 0.85 ('well-structured'); GroundEval scored it 0.000 because the agent never fetched the required artifact. Framework is training-free and model-agnostic. Only validated on a synthetic corporate sim (OrgForge), not tau2/AppWorld.
methods.limitation: You must author a machine-readable contract per task (upfront cost); demonstrated only on one synthetic domain with a single case-study number, no aggregate AUROC.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable and training-free. tau2 tasks already ship golden action sequences + target state that can be compiled into GroundEval-style contracts; the pattern is exactly 'did the required tool calls actually happen against real returns' which catches fabricated-confirmation entries.

methods.name: Agent-Diff — canonical state-diff evaluation via code execution
methods.one_liner: Verify only the before/after state DIFF (inserted / deleted / updated entities across all tables), computed by executing against real APIs, ignoring the agent's action trace entirely.
methods.mechanism: Executes code against real enterprise APIs and captures the resulting state change as a canonical diff of inserted/deleted/updated entities across all tables; all verification is performed on the diff, not on the trace. Multiple valid action paths that reach the same correct diff all pass; a plausible-looking trace that produced no diff fails.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: judges actual persisted state change, robust to path variation and to eloquent-but-inert transcripts; (2) admission: diff match is deterministic.
methods.evidence: Benchmark on enterprise API tasks (Box, Google Calendar, Linear, Slack); framing/design shows state-diff grounding over trajectory- or LLM-judge scoring. Training-free evaluation framework. (PDF body numbers not extractable.)
methods.limitation: Needs a real/executable backend and a reference diff per task; robustness to 'equivalent but differently-represented' states depends on the canonicalization.
methods.portable_to_us: Directly portable — this is essentially the tau2/AppWorld state-check philosophy generalized. Admit an experience only if replaying/executing its recommended actions yields the reference state diff; kills entries that teach non-acting shortcuts.

methods.name: OpenComputer — execution-grounded verifier + self-evolving verifier refinement loop
methods.one_liner: Programmatic state-inspection verifiers plus a self-evolving loop where LLM-judge-vs-verifier DISAGREEMENT is diagnosed to iteratively repair the verifier — a template for bootstrapping trustworthy gates.
methods.mechanism: App-specific verifiers expose structured JSON state via reliable inspection channels (browser debug protocol, D-Bus, SQLite profile DBs, direct file parsing) and are unit/integration-tested before use. A self-evolving loop runs calibration tasks through strong agents, has both an LLM evaluator and the programmatic verifier score final state, categorizes every disagreement as verifier-error vs agent-failure, and applies bounded debug-fix-retry only to verifier code (trajectories are frozen).
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: programmatic state inspection beats screenshot/transcript reading; (2) admission: verifier verdicts, not judge prose; (3) source: the disagreement loop is a mechanism to BUILD a reliable gate where you only have an LLM judge today.
methods.evidence: Programmatic verifier vs LLM-judge human agreement: 97.3% vs 79.2% task-level (94.1% vs 92.2% checklist-level); the gap widens on dense/terminal workflows where 'near-miss' outputs fool screenshot inspection. Self-evolution: 89.4% repair rate, human-checker agreement 85.2%->94.1% (+8.9pp), 47/68 errors fixed in one iteration. Training-free (uses GPT-5.4 / Sonnet-4.6).
methods.limitation: Building per-app inspection endpoints is engineering-heavy; demonstrated on desktop/GUI apps (OSWorld-style), not tau2 directly.
methods.portable_to_us: Highly relevant conceptually. tau2/AppWorld already expose DB state (the 'inspection channel'), so the valuable transfer is the disagreement-diagnosis self-evolving loop: use the ground-truth checker to find where your admission judge disagrees, and refine the judge/verifier prompt+checks against those cases. Training-free.

methods.name: Vera — evidence-grounded verification hierarchy (state ⊳ tool-log ⊳ response)
methods.one_liner: A strict priority order for verification: check persisted environment STATE first, then tool-call LOGS, and only then the agent's self-reported response — because a tool call records intent, not effect.
methods.mechanism: Deterministic Python verifiers V(τ,sT) composed as V_state(sT) ⊳ V_tool(τ) ⊳ V_resp(τ): environment state is checked first (did the outcome actually persist), then tool-call logs (what was executed), then agent claims (what it said). Explicitly designed to prevent false positives (verbal compliance after real harm) and false negatives (claimed refusal despite side effects).
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: formalizes 'state > logs > words' as the trust ordering; (2) admission: verifiers are deterministic replayed Python, not LLM judgment; (3) source: catches say-one-thing-do-another entries.
methods.evidence: 1,600 executable test cases across 124 risk categories / 30 environments (email, code hosting, payment, messaging, web search), 4 production agents. Verifiers are deterministic Python generated once and replayed. Training-free. (No direct state-vs-transcript ablation AUROC given; benign 70.5% vs attack 90.6-93.9% gap reflects the stricter multi-predicate grounding.)
methods.limitation: No clean isolated ablation quantifying state-grounding's AUROC lift; verifier authoring per task.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable as a design principle you can adopt immediately: in your admission check, query tau2/AppWorld DB state first, fall back to tool-call logs, and treat the agent's success claim as the weakest evidence. Directly counters the 'fabricate confirmation number' cheat.

methods.name: Tool receipts — cross-check claimed tool calls against the execution log
methods.one_liner: The cheapest possible grounding: every tool call the agent CLAIMS to have made must match an entry in the actual execution log; a claim with no log entry is a fabricated execution, flag and reject.
methods.mechanism: Cross-reference each claimed action against the runtime tool-call log; if the agent says 'I processed the refund / I checked the DB' but there is no corresponding logged successful call, auto-flag as hallucination. Optional hardening: tools return unforgeable cryptographically-signed receipts the agent must present. Related multi-strategy protocol adds schema validation, computation replay, temporal consistency, and cross-source corroboration.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: uses the execution log as the only source of truth; (3) source: directly detects the exact cheat in your diagnosis (entries that claim success/confirmation numbers without acting).
methods.evidence: Widely-adopted practical pattern (Tool Receipts paper 2603.10060; industry hallucination-detection writeups). Near-zero cost, deterministic. (Paper's precise precision/recall not extractable from the PDF stream, but mechanism is a deterministic set-membership check so precision is essentially perfect for outright-fabricated calls.)
methods.limitation: Only catches claims that have NO matching log entry (pure fabrication); it does not verify that a real tool call achieved the correct semantic outcome (that needs M1/M3/M5). Weak against calls that executed but with wrong args/effect.
methods.portable_to_us: Extremely portable, training-free, near-free. As a first-pass admission filter over tau2/AppWorld experience candidates: reject any candidate whose narrative asserts an action absent from the logged tool calls. Pair with M1 for semantic correctness.

methods.name: VAGEN / Agentic Reward Modeling — proactive-probing verifier ('easy to verify, hard to solve')
methods.one_liner: Give the judge tools and let it ACTIVELY probe the environment after the episode — run shell/Python/queries to uncover hidden state — instead of passively reading the terminal output.
methods.mechanism: Progressive verification in escalating stages: (1) static assessment of final output/summary, (2) visual/log retrospection over historical snapshots, (3) proactive probing that deploys shell commands, Python execution, and direct GUI/API interaction to surface hidden system state. Exploits the asymmetry that outcomes are far easier to verify by inspection than to produce.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: judge inspects real post-hoc environment state via its own tool calls; (2) admission: verdict rests on probed evidence, not the agent's narration.
methods.evidence: OSWorld-Verified accuracy 84.7%->92.9% (class-balanced) and 85.3%->93.4% (imbalanced) vs LLM-as-judge; AndroidWorld 93.1% vs 87.9% next-best. Ablation: removing the environment-interaction tools collapses accuracy to 78.5%, proving proactive probing is doing the work. Training-free (prompting + tool augmentation over frontier models).
methods.limitation: Requires the judge to have live read access to the environment at admission time; probing adds latency/cost per candidate.
methods.portable_to_us: Strong fit and training-free. Turn your admission judge into an agent with read-only tools over the tau2/AppWorld DB: after an episode, let it query the tables to confirm the claimed booking/refund/write actually exists before admitting the experience. The +8 to +14pp lifts here are the closest analog to the gain you need.

methods.name: Online Agent-as-a-Judge — situation-generating (interactive) evaluation
methods.one_liner: The judge generates fresh follow-up situations/probes and interacts with the environment to test whether the goal was truly achieved, rather than scoring a static transcript.
methods.mechanism: Instead of passive transcript review, an LLM judge formulates targeted follow-up scenarios and test cases and re-engages the environment/agent to confirm the claimed outcome holds under probing, catching cases where the agent 'appeared successful but hadn't genuinely completed the objective.'
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: active environment interaction; (2) admission: verdict from probe outcomes rather than self-report.
methods.evidence: Reports significantly higher correlation with ground-truth outcomes and reduced false positives vs passive transcript review, across web / OS / game / social-simulation domains. Training-free (existing LLMs, no fine-tuning).
methods.limitation: Generated probe situations can themselves be off-target; needs a resettable/queryable environment; more expensive than a single judge pass.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable where you can re-enter the tau2/AppWorld environment. Use it as a second-stage gate on candidates that pass cheap checks: have the judge issue confirmation queries (e.g., 'list this user's active reservations') and admit only if state matches the claim. Training-free.

methods.name: Agentic Rubrics as Contextual Verifiers
methods.one_liner: Let the judge use tool calls (search, inspection) to bind each rubric item to CONCRETE environment entities (specific rows/files/tool calls) so criteria are checkable facts, not vague high-level statements that invite false positives.
methods.mechanism: The verifier issues targeted tool calls to ground rubric items in concrete repo/environment entities (files, classes, methods — analogously DB rows, order IDs, tool-call args). Grounded items ('order 123 status == cancelled') are unambiguously gradable; ungrounded high-level items ('touches the right code path') are the ones that produce false positives.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: rubric items reference verifiable entities pulled from the environment; (2) admission: reduces the ambiguity the judge exploits to rubber-stamp.
methods.evidence: On SWE-Bench Verified, removing repository access from the rubric generator drops BEST@16 by 4.0 pts (Qwen3-32B) and 1.4 pts (Qwen3-Coder-30B), i.e., agentic context-gathering measurably improves selection and cuts false positives. Training-free post-hoc verification layer.
methods.limitation: Demonstrated on SWE agents; rubric authoring/generation cost; grounding quality depends on the verifier's tool-use skill.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable and training-free. For tau2/AppWorld, auto-generate rubric items that name concrete target entities (the golden tool calls / target-state rows) and require the judge to fetch and confirm each before admitting. Converts a fuzzy 'did it succeed?' into checkable predicates.

methods.name: Execute-Distill-Verify (EDV) — escaping the self-confirmation trap in experience learning
methods.one_liner: The published work closest to your exact problem: it names the 'self-confirmation trap' (LLM judge admits plausible-but-fake experience entries) and gates admission on execution-group consensus, tested on tau2-bench.
methods.mechanism: Three stages: Execute (attempt task in the real environment), Distill (summarize the trace into a candidate experience), Verify (an execution GROUP validates candidates via a consensus mechanism grounded in re-execution / ground-truth environment state rather than a single transcript judge; only approved experiences are written into shared or private memory).
methods.addresses: (2) admission: replaces the foolable single-judge filter with execution-grounded group consensus; (3) source: only verified experiences enter memory, blocking corrupting/fake entries.
methods.evidence: Reports EDV 'consistently outperforms strong baselines' on tau2-bench, Mind2Web, and MMTB (long-horizon constraint-solving, web, multi-tool). Verify stage explicitly validates via execution-group consensus and writes only approved experiences to memory. (Exact success-delta numbers not extractable from the PDF/abstract; 28-page paper, arXiv 2606.24428.)
methods.limitation: Could not extract precise deltas or a stated false-admission-reduction number; consensus adds compute; verification fidelity depends on re-execution being available.
methods.portable_to_us: The single most directly applicable method — same task (experience library), same benchmark (τ²-bench), same failure (self-confirmation). Read the full PDF (saved locally) for the exact Verify protocol and reuse it. Appears training-free / API-based. Treat as prior art to build on and differentiate from.

methods.name: 'Silent Failure' structural detector + the empirical case for state grounding
methods.one_liner: Your empirical anchor: it independently measures the tau2 judge AUROC ceiling at 0.65 and offers a 3300x-cheaper structural detector as triage — while concluding only direct trajectory-environment consistency checks suffice.
methods.mechanism: Characterizes false success (agent claims completion without acting / fabricates confirmations) and trains a lightweight TF-IDF + XGBoost classifier on trajectory surface features as a fast triage detector; explicitly recommends reserving direct trajectory-environment consistency checks for higher-stakes decisions.
methods.addresses: (2) admission: a cheap triage signal to pre-filter candidates; also validates why (1) grounding is necessary by quantifying the transcript-judge ceiling.
methods.evidence: False-success prevalence: 45-48% of failures on tau2 airline/retail, 75.8% on AppWorld. Transcript LLM-judge ceiling: AUROC 0.65 tau2 (Sonnet-4.5) / 0.54 AppWorld — reasoning models don't help (DeepSeek-R1 0.573); judges anchor on 'confident closing language' and 'action-sequence volume'. Structural detector task-disjoint AUROC 0.825 (tau2) / 0.953 (AppWorld), cross-model 0.913; at 10% flag rate 72% recall / 50% precision; 1.19 ms/inference (~3300x faster than LLM judges).
methods.limitation: The detector needs labeled trajectories to train (not fully training-free) and 50% precision is 'insufficient for autonomous deployment' alone; authors state neither structural detection nor LLM-judge suffices without direct state verification.
methods.portable_to_us: Directly usable on tau2/AppWorld: cite it as the quantitative backbone of your diagnosis (its 0.65 ceiling == your '<0.65'), and deploy the cheap detector as a pre-filter that routes suspicious candidates to expensive state verification (M1/M7). Not the final gate, but a cost-efficient first pass.

methods.name: Process reward / step-level verification for tool agents (ToolPRM-style)
methods.one_liner: Verify admission at the STEP level — score whether each tool call verifiably advanced the environment state — instead of a single end-of-trajectory verdict, localizing where a plausible trace actually went inert.
methods.mechanism: Convert a trajectory into step-level test cases and score each step's correctness; the strongest variants have the process verifier interact with the environment to validate a step against real data rather than judging it from text. Enables reward-guided search and fine-grained error localization.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: per-step checks can query real tool outputs; (2) admission: admit/keep only the sub-sequence of steps that verifiably progress state, discarding the fabricated tail.
methods.evidence: ToolPRMBench builds step-level test cases over multiple tool-use benchmarks; PRMs shown to give finer error localization than outcome-only reward and to interact with the environment for validation.
methods.limitation: Most PRMs are trained models (not training-free); as an LLM-prompted step-checker it is training-free but weaker; step-level ground truth is harder to obtain than final-state ground truth.
methods.portable_to_us: Partially portable. Training-free variant: prompt the judge to verify, per step, that the claimed tool output matches the actual logged return before distilling that step into the library. Useful for admitting partial/step-level experiences rather than whole trajectories on AppWorld's long-horizon tasks.

methods.name: Proxy state-based evaluation — LLM state-tracker reconstructs DB state from the trajectory
methods.one_liner: When you can't call a real ground-truth backend at admission time, an LLM 'state tracker' reconstructs a proxy environment state from the full trajectory prefix and judges goal-completion + hallucination against THAT, which is far stronger than raw transcript reading.
methods.mechanism: A dedicated state-tracker component infers proxy state s̃ = f_state(τ) conditioned on the entire interaction prefix: seeds initial facts, applies read/write semantics of each tool call (writes commit only on success confirmation), and tool simulators read from this proxy state. Separate goal-completion and hallucination judges then score against the reconstructed state rather than prose.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding: an explicit reconstructed state object mediates judgment, decoupling 'what state resulted' from 'what the agent said'; (2) admission: hallucination judge specifically flags claims inconsistent with tracked state.
methods.evidence: Human-judge three-way agreement: 82.7% goal completion, 94.7% tool-hallucination, 94.7% user-hallucination; intermediate-state validation 92% correct (IAA 0.86); tool-hallucination rate 1.33%±0.53. Built for exactly the commerce/account (tau-like) domains. API-based (5 LLM components: GPT-5o/Gemini).
methods.limitation: Proxy state is still LLM-INFERRED, not true ground truth — weaker than M1/M3 when the real checker is available; multi-component and thus more expensive; deliberately not tau-bench-compatible in its native form.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable as a fallback grounding layer when the real evaluator can't be invoked (e.g., experiences mined from logs without target states). For tau2/AppWorld offline building you should prefer M1; use this style only to strengthen the judge when ground truth is unavailable.

methods.name: Iterative reward calibration (MT-GRPO/GTPO) — grounding reward against execution outcomes
methods.one_liner: Included as an execution-grounding contrast that is NOT training-free: it calibrates the reward against whether tool calls actually succeeded in the environment, so the policy can't learn to exploit fake-success reward on tau-bench.
methods.mechanism: After each action, recalibrates the reward signal using observed state changes / execution outcomes, comparing predicted reward against ground-truth execution results; fake success (claims completion without executing) is detected and penalized. Combines multi-turn GRPO with group-think preference optimization to co-refine policy and reward model.
methods.addresses: (2) admission: shows the general principle of grounding the acceptance signal in execution outcomes rather than an appearance-based reward.
methods.evidence: Evaluated on TAU-Bench with multiple benchmark tables showing calibration improving over iterations; core claim is that comparing predicted reward to ground-truth execution results catches and penalizes fake success. (Precise deltas not extractable.)
methods.limitation: RL-training-dependent (PPO-style iterations to co-train policy + reward) — NOT training-free, so it violates your API-model preference; useful mainly as conceptual confirmation.
methods.portable_to_us: Low direct portability (requires RL training). Include only as evidence that the field independently converges on 'ground the acceptance signal in real execution outcomes'; the training-free analog of its insight is M1/M3 (compare claimed reward to actual state).

grounding.takeaway: The literature converges hard on your diagnosis and points to one dominant fix: stop letting an LLM read the transcript and instead gate admission on real environment state. The 'Silent Failure' study (arXiv 2606.09863) independently reproduces your exact number — transcript-judge AUROC tops out at 0.65 on tau2 / 0.54 on AppWorld because judges anchor on 'confident closing language,' with 45-76% of failures being fabricated success — and concludes that only direct trajectory-environment consistency checks are sufficient. For a training-free experience-library optimizer on tau2/AppWorld, the highest-ROI, near-zero-cost move is M1: use each benchmark's OWN programmatic evaluator (tau2 DB-state hash comparison; AppWorld state-based unit tests with collateral-damage checks) as the admission gate — this is the identical signal that gave your oracle library +9.4pp, and it makes the fabricate-a-confirmation-number cheat score 0. Layer three cheap training-free reinforcements on top: (a) tool-receipt cross-checking (M6) to reject any candidate claiming an action absent from the tool-call log; (b) a proactive-probing agent-judge with read-only DB access (M7/M8, the pattern that buys +8 to +14pp elsewhere) for candidates mined without ground-truth labels; and (c) the state ⊳ tool-log ⊳ response trust ordering (M5) as your verification design principle. Two works are must-reads because they target your exact setting: Execute-Distill-Verify (M10, arXiv 2606.24428) names the 'self-confirmation trap' and gates memory admission via execution-group consensus on τ²-bench — the closest prior art to differentiate against — and OpenComputer (M4) supplies a reusable, training-free self-evolving loop that uses LLM-judge-vs-programmatic-verifier disagreement to bootstrap and refine a trustworthy gate (97.3% vs 79.2% human agreement for programmatic over LLM judging). Net: the trustworthy admission signal you need already exists in-environment; the research contribution is engineering the gate (and, where ground truth is unavailable, reconstructing proxy state via M13) rather than trying to make a transcript-only judge smarter, which the evidence says has a hard ceiling.

methods.name: Execution-grounded / proxy-state verifier (state-tracker judge)
methods.one_liner: Replace the transcript-reading judge with a verifier that reconstructs backend/DB state from the tool-call trace and checks it against the expected final state.
methods.mechanism: An LLM 'state tracker' consumes the full trace (dialogue + tool calls + tool outputs) and emits a structured proxy of what the backend SHOULD now contain; a judge compares that inferred state against the scenario's expected final state and behavior constraints, rather than scoring conversation semantics. Proxy State-Based Evaluation (arXiv 2602.16246) formalizes this with cooperating components (agent, user sim, tool sims, state tracker, judge). Admission then gates on 'did the required state change happen', not 'does the transcript read like success'.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding the judge with execution evidence — this IS the grounding mechanism; also enables (3) because only state-verified trajectories can seed an entry.
methods.evidence: Human-LLM judge agreement >90%; three-way agreement 82.7% goal-completion, 94.7% tool-hallucination, 94.7% user-hallucination on 50 sampled convs; tool/user hallucination driven to 1.33%/0.67%. This is the exact fix for our failure mode (judge cannot see whether DB state changed).
methods.limitation: State tracker is itself an LLM inference from the trace, so it can still be fooled where the trace omits the decisive side-effect; strongest when the environment exposes true state (then use real reward, not the proxy).
methods.portable_to_us: Highly portable and training-free. tau2-bench and AppWorld BOTH ship deterministic state and a verifiable reward — so you can skip the proxy and gate admission directly on the ground-truth environment reward of the seeding rollout; use the proxy-state judge only where you cannot reset/inspect state. Near-zero extra cost since the env already computes reward.

methods.name: Dual-control / independent state-verification as structural admission gate
methods.one_liner: Require a process independent of the agent (env, user simulator, or a second agent) to confirm the claimed state change before the entry is admitted.
methods.mechanism: Instead of trusting the agent's self-report or a judge that only reads what the agent wrote, an independent controller re-checks the environment state after the trajectory. Characterizing False Success (arXiv 2606.09863) shows tau2-telecom is 'dual-control' (the user simulator can independently verify state) whereas airline/retail are single-control. Admission = admit only trajectories whose success is confirmed by the independent verifier.
methods.addresses: (2) admission mechanism not relying on a foolable judge — it is a structural, not judgmental, gate; also (3).
methods.evidence: Single-control domains: false success = 45-48% of failures. Dual-control (telecom): 3% — a 15x reduction. Paper explicitly calls independent verification 'a promising structural defense against silent agent failure'. Directly quantifies exactly our problem.
methods.limitation: Requires the environment to support independent verification; for read-only-then-claim tasks you must add a verifier that re-queries state. Not applicable if no side-effect is observable.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable and training-free. In tau2 use the user-simulator/DB as the independent checker; in AppWorld the deterministic backend already verifies. The design rule for us: never admit an entry whose seeding success was self-declared — only entries whose success was confirmed by the env's own state check.

methods.name: Learned lightweight triage detector (features → false-success classifier)
methods.one_liner: Train a cheap classifier on trajectory features to flag likely-fake successes for review, catching far more than an LLM judge at equal budget.
methods.mechanism: TF-IDF (or structured trajectory features) + XGBoost predicts whether a claimed-success trajectory is actually false. Used as a triage layer: rank candidate entries by suspicion, human/grounded-check the top-k, admit the rest. From Characterizing False Success (arXiv 2606.09863).
methods.addresses: (2) admission mechanism — a non-LLM-judge gate; (1) partially, since it exploits execution-trace features the judge ignores.
methods.evidence: AUROC 0.83 on tau2, 0.95 on AppWorld; recovers 4-8x MORE false successes than the best LLM judge at equal review budget; 3300x lower latency than an LLM judge. Beats the judge on our exact benchmarks.
methods.limitation: Needs a small labeled set of true/false successes to train (so NOT fully training-free); precision at practical operating points ~50%, so it is a triage/ranking signal that feeds a grounded check or human, not an autonomous admitter.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable at low cost: label a few hundred tau2/AppWorld trajectories using the env's ground-truth reward (free labels!), fit XGBoost, use it to prioritize which candidate entries get the expensive grounded/counterfactual check. Cheap, and the 'training' data is auto-labeled by the environment.

methods.name: Held-out validation gate on real task success (GEPA/DSPy-style)
methods.one_liner: Admit a candidate entry only if it raises aggregate task success on a disjoint held-out validation set of real rollouts — never on a judge score.
methods.mechanism: Split tasks into a feedback/mutation pool and a held-out validation pool. Entries are PROPOSED from feedback trajectories but ACCEPTED only if the library-with-entry scores higher on the held-out pool's actual environment reward. GEPA (arXiv 2507.19457) maintains a Pareto frontier of candidates (best on at least one instance) so complementary strategies are retained and you don't overfit one metric.
methods.addresses: (2) admission mechanism using actual task outcome, not judge score — the single cleanest replacement for the foolable judge; also (3).
methods.evidence: GEPA outperforms MIPROv2 by ~13% and GRPO by ~20% with 35x fewer rollouts by selecting on held-out validation performance; the train/val split is explicitly what prevents accepting 'sampling luck'. Established, ICLR-2026-oral method.
methods.limitation: Costs validation rollouts per candidate; small validation sets create multiple-comparison / variance risk (mitigate with L2-08/09/10). Requires a reliable per-task reward — which tau2/AppWorld provide.
methods.portable_to_us: The highest-value, lowest-effort move for us and fully training-free. tau2/AppWorld both expose deterministic verifiable reward, so a held-out validation gate on real success rate is a drop-in replacement for the LLM-judge admission signal. This is the direct operationalization of 'use actual task outcome not judge score'.

methods.name: Counterfactual A/B (with-vs-without) acceptance and leave-one-out pruning
methods.one_liner: Accept an entry only if running the agent WITH it beats running WITHOUT it on a validation batch by a margin; periodically prune entries by their marginal contribution.
methods.mechanism: For candidate entry e, execute the agent on a validation batch with library L and with library L+{e}; admit iff measured env reward improves beyond a margin/noise band. Symmetrically, run leave-one-out: drop each existing entry, measure the reward change, and evict entries whose marginal utility is <=0 (this catches the cheat entries that teach fabrication — they will not raise, and often lower, true task reward). MemRL (arXiv 2601.03192) retains by learned Q-value/utility rather than semantic similarity; ReasoningBank mines counterfactual pitfall signals.
methods.addresses: (2) counterfactual acceptance grounded in outcome; (3) actively purges harmful/cheat entries via negative marginal utility.
methods.evidence: Ablation/counterfactual retention is standard and effective (AgeMem full-vs-ablation +14-22%; MemRL utility-based retrieval beats similarity). Directly measures the causal effect of the entry on true reward — a cheat entry that fakes confirmation numbers cannot pass because env reward stays 0.
methods.limitation: O(batch x candidates) rollouts; noisy for small batches (pair with variance control L2-10/11); interactions between entries mean marginal utility is context-dependent.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and directly portable; the env reward is the A/B metric. Budget-controllable by A/B-ing only on a small stratified validation slice. This plus L2-04 covers the two 'outcome-grounded acceptance' families the task named.

methods.name: Ensemble / LLM jury (PoLL) with disagreement gating
methods.one_liner: Replace the single judge with a panel of diverse model families; admit on agreement, escalate/abstain on disagreement.
methods.mechanism: Panel of LLMs (PoLL, arXiv 2404.18796) from disjoint families independently score; aggregate by vote/mean. Disagreement is treated as signal: unanimous-accept admits, split routes to a grounded check or human. Diversity dilutes any single model's systematic bias / correlated blind spot.
methods.addresses: (2) admission not relying on ONE judge; provides the disagreement trigger for human-in-the-loop (L2-13).
methods.evidence: PoLL of 3 small models correlates with humans BETTER than a single GPT-4 judge, with less intra-model bias, at 7-8x lower cost. Good variance/bias reduction and cheap.
methods.limitation: Provably insufficient alone against reward hacking: when the generator finds a pathological template that MOST evaluators endorse, the whole panel agrees and is wrong (documented in the ensemble literature). So the panel cannot fix a SHARED blind spot — and 'fabricate a confirmation number' is exactly the kind of transcript-plausible cheat that fools correlated LLM judges. Use as cheap first-stage triage, never as the sole grounded gate.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and trivial to add, but must be layered ON TOP of an outcome-grounded gate (L2-01/04/05). Its real value for us is producing the disagreement signal that decides which entries get the expensive grounded/human check.

methods.name: Multi-agent debate / adversarial cross-examination verification
methods.one_liner: A prosecutor and defender argue whether the goal was truly achieved; admit only on adversarial-survival / unanimous verdict.
methods.mechanism: Assign adversarial roles (one argues the trajectory faked success, one defends), run 1-2 rounds of cross-examination that force each side to cite concrete trace evidence, then a verdict. Unanimous-agreement or AB/BA-order-consistent voting collapses ambiguous cases to reject. Transforms static scoring into dynamic verification that surfaces hallucinated 'success'.
methods.addresses: (2) admission via adversarial verification rather than a single pass; can force execution-evidence citation, touching (1).
methods.evidence: Some settings report multi-agent judges reaching near-perfect precision / eliminating false-positive rewards, and unanimous-vote schemes 'drastically reduce false-positive rates and self-hallucinations'.
methods.limitation: Documented failure modes: sycophancy and disagreement-collapse can make debate WORSE than a single agent (agents converge on a confident wrong answer); hallucination can amplify across agents. Needs anonymized identities and a payoff that rewards correctness over agreement to work.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and portable, but fragile; only worth it if debaters are REQUIRED to ground claims in the tool-call trace / state diff (otherwise it inherits the transcript-only blind spot). Best combined with L2-01 so the debate argues over verified state, not prose.

methods.name: Conformal selective admission with abstention (SCOPE-style)
methods.one_liner: Calibrate a nonconformity threshold so admitted entries have a provably bounded error rate; abstain (defer/discard) when confidence is below threshold.
methods.mechanism: On a small labeled calibration set (exchangeable with deployment), compute nonconformity scores for the judge's accept decisions; pick a threshold giving target coverage. At runtime admit only when the score clears the threshold, otherwise ABSTAIN and route to human or drop. SCOPE (Selective Conformal Optimized Pairwise LLM Judging, arXiv 2602.13110) does exactly this for pairwise LLM judging; general conformal-abstention frameworks give the guarantee.
methods.addresses: (2) admission with a finite-sample statistical guarantee and a principled abstain option instead of forcing a fooled yes/no.
methods.evidence: Conformal abstention gives distribution-free finite-sample control of the error rate on non-abstained items; SCOPE specifically targets selective pairwise judging with coverage guarantees. Turns 'AUROC<0.65 judge' into 'admit only the high-confidence tail where the judge IS trustworthy'.
methods.limitation: Guarantee is only on the CALIBRATED score (if the judge is uniformly fooled by a cheat class, calibration on clean data won't catch it); needs a labeled, exchangeable calibration set; controls error on admitted set but can abstain heavily when the judge is weak.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free given a small calibration set auto-labeled by the env reward. Concretely: calibrate the judge on env-verified tau2/AppWorld trajectories, then only auto-admit entries in the confident region and send the rest to L2-05 counterfactual A/B or a human.

methods.name: Conformal risk / FDR-controlled batch admission
methods.one_liner: When admitting a batch of candidate entries, control the expected fraction of false admissions (FDR) below a target using conformal p-values.
methods.mechanism: Compute a conformal p-value (or conformal q-value) per candidate entry against a null of 'no true improvement', then apply a Benjamini-Hochberg-style / conformal-q-value procedure to admit the largest set whose expected false-discovery proportion <= target alpha. Handles the multiple-comparisons problem created by testing many candidate entries at once. Based on FDR-controlled conformal selection (arXiv 2311.15322) and conformal selective prediction with general risk control (arXiv 2603.24704).
methods.addresses: (2) admission as a calibrated multiple-testing decision — controls the RATE of bad entries entering per batch, not just per-item.
methods.evidence: Conformal q-value / FDR methods give finite-sample control of expected false-discovery proportion under exchangeability, including structured/asymmetric settings. Fixes the failure where testing thousands of candidate entries against a noisy signal guarantees some false admits (Bonferroni/FDR is the correct lens).
methods.limitation: Requires per-candidate scores exchangeable with a calibration set; FDR is an average guarantee (a specific bad entry can still slip). Assumes the score has SOME signal for true improvement — get that from L2-05's measured reward delta, not the judge.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free wrapper around any per-entry score (best: the counterfactual reward delta from L2-05). Right tool for us precisely because a self-evolving library proposes MANY entries per iteration; FDR control caps how many cheats accumulate over time.

methods.name: Pessimistic / lower-confidence-bound (LCB) admission via off-policy evaluation
methods.one_liner: Estimate the value of library-with-entry from logged trajectories and admit only if its empirical LOWER bound exceeds the current library's value.
methods.mechanism: Adopt the pessimism principle: for each candidate library change, form an importance-weighted value estimate from logged rollouts and its high-probability lower confidence bound; admit only if LCB(L+{e}) > value(L). Logarithmic Smoothing (NeurIPS 2024, arXiv 2405.14335) provides the tightest fully-empirical sub-Gaussian concentration bounds in this class and yields improved selection/learning by logarithmically smoothing large importance weights.
methods.addresses: (2) admission via a worst-case lower bound — structurally refuses to admit an entry whose apparent gain is within statistical noise, which is exactly how cheat entries sneak past a point-estimate judge.
methods.evidence: LS bound provably tighter than IPS/clipped competitors, sub-Gaussian rate, finite variance without boundedness; pessimistic selection is the standard cure for over-optimistic acceptance. Reframes admission as 'is the improvement real beyond noise', not 'did a judge say yes'.
methods.limitation: Importance weights can have high variance / poor overlap if the new library shifts behavior a lot; bound can be loose (over-conservative, admits little). Needs logged action probabilities or a proxy.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free statistical layer over the rollout logs we already collect. Especially valuable to reuse OLD trajectories so you don't re-run rollouts for every candidate (see L2-12); combine with L2-04/05 to turn their noisy reward deltas into calibrated accept/reject.

methods.name: Offline-RL safe policy improvement bound (HCPI / SPIBB)
methods.one_liner: Treat each library edit as a policy change and admit only if it is guaranteed, with probability >= 1-delta, to be no worse than the current library — else keep the baseline.
methods.mechanism: High Confidence Policy Improvement (Thomas 2015) returns a new policy only when an importance-sampling lower bound certifies performance above a user-set floor at confidence 1-delta, else returns the baseline. Safe Policy Improvement with Baseline Bootstrapping (Laroche 2019) restricts changes to state-actions with sufficient data coverage and falls back to the baseline elsewhere. Map: baseline policy = current library, candidate policy = library+edit; only certified-improving edits are admitted.
methods.addresses: (2) admission with a formal 'never regress' guarantee and an explicit fallback — prevents the monotone degradation we observe when cheat entries accumulate.
methods.evidence: HCPI/SPIBB give probabilistic guarantees that the accepted change does not underperform the baseline; SPIBB's coverage threshold is exactly the mechanism to refuse edits supported by too little evidence. Well-established, theoretically grounded.
methods.limitation: Guarantees are w.r.t. the LOGGED reward distribution — need a trustworthy reward (env reward, not judge); conservative (rejects many valid edits under low data); classic SPI assumes tabular-ish structure so the mapping to text libraries is conceptual, implemented via the OPE bounds of L2-10.
methods.portable_to_us: Conceptually the exact frame for our problem (self-evolution = repeated safe policy improvement) and training-free. Practical instantiation for us = L2-10's LCB gate + a 'coverage' rule (don't admit entries whose target situations are underrepresented in validation).

methods.name: Importance-weighted (IPS / SNIPS / doubly-robust) counterfactual acceptance
methods.one_liner: Reuse existing logged trajectories to estimate the counterfactual task-success of the modified library, avoiding fresh rollouts for every candidate.
methods.mechanism: Estimate value(L+{e}) off-policy from already-collected trajectories via inverse-propensity weighting, self-normalized IPS (SNIPS), or a doubly-robust estimator that combines a reward model with IPS correction; admit if the counterfactual estimate (or its LCB) beats the current library. Standard OPE toolkit (truncated IPS, SNIPS, DR).
methods.addresses: (2) admission from counterfactual outcome estimates computed on logs rather than a judge, at much lower rollout cost.
methods.evidence: IPS/SNIPS/DR are the workhorse of off-policy evaluation for recommender/bandit systems; DR reduces variance and bias vs plain IPS. Lets us test many candidate entries cheaply against real logged outcomes.
methods.limitation: High/unbounded variance under poor overlap or large behavior shift; needs behavior-policy propensities (approximate for LLMs) or a good reward model for the DR term; biased if the modified library changes the action distribution drastically.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and the cost-saver that makes L2-05/L2-10 affordable at library scale: score candidates on logged tau2/AppWorld rollouts first, then spend fresh rollouts only on the survivors. Pair with LS/clipping (L2-10) to tame variance.

methods.name: Learning-to-defer / predictor-rejector + human-in-the-loop only on disagreement
methods.one_liner: The gate learns when to auto-admit vs defer to a human, minimizing a cost that trades human-review budget against false-admission cost; route only ambiguous cases to humans.
methods.mechanism: A predictor-rejector / L2D setup: a rejector decides per candidate whether to auto-decide or defer to a human expert, trained to minimize a cost-sensitive objective (false-admission cost vs human cost, with workload constraints). Trigger for deferral can be jury disagreement (L2-06), low conformal confidence (L2-08), or small/uncertain reward delta (L2-05). Cost-Sensitive L2D to multiple experts (arXiv 2403.06906); predictor-rejector abstention theory (arXiv 2310.14772).
methods.addresses: (2) admission that explicitly incorporates a human fallback and allocates it where the automatic gate is least trustworthy, instead of blindly trusting a judge.
methods.evidence: L2D provably improves over selective classification by modeling expert error and cost; cost-sensitive L2D handles per-class costs and reviewer workload budgets. Concentrates scarce human oversight exactly on the disagreement/low-confidence tail where AUROC<0.65 judges fail.
methods.limitation: Requires SOME human-review budget; the learned rejector needs data to train (though the routing rule can be a simple training-free heuristic: defer on jury split or |reward delta|<margin); humans are also fallible on long tool-use transcripts.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable in its heuristic form with zero training: auto-admit only entries that BOTH pass a grounded outcome check (L2-01/04/05) AND have panel agreement, and human-review the rest. Gives a scalable-oversight admission pipeline where humans touch only a few percent of candidates.

methods.name: Self-consistency reproduction gate (stochastic re-execution)
methods.one_liner: Admit an entry only if applying it reproduces task success across k independently resampled rollouts, not a single transcript.
methods.mechanism: Before admission, apply the candidate entry and re-run the associated task k times with sampling; admit only if success is reproduced (e.g., >= m/k) under the env reward. Turns a one-off plausible transcript into a repeated-trial test, killing entries that 'worked once' by luck or by transcript-only plausibility.
methods.addresses: (2) admission via repeated-trial evidence; (3) filters flaky/lucky/cheat entries at the source.
methods.evidence: Self-consistency / majority-vote over resampled rollouts is a robust, well-studied test-time signal (self-consistency CoT +~18% over vanilla in reasoning). Cheaply raises the bar an entry must clear.
methods.limitation: CRITICAL: consistency != correctness — a cheat entry that RELIABLY fabricates a confirmation number will reproduce a 'plausible success' every time. So this MUST be scored by the environment reward, not by the judge/transcript; otherwise it entrenches a reliable-but-wrong strategy. Also costs k rollouts per candidate.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and portable, but only safe when the k re-executions are graded by tau2/AppWorld's real reward. Under real reward it becomes a variance-reduced version of L2-05; without real reward it is dangerous for our exact failure mode.

methods.name: Rubric/checklist verification decomposed into grounded, machine-checkable predicates
methods.one_liner: Replace free-form judging with a decomposed rubric of verifiable checks tied to the tool trace and state schema (did tool X fire with args Y; did field Z change).
methods.mechanism: For each task, derive from the environment's own state/API schema a checklist of atomic, verifiable predicates (specific tool called, specific argument present, specific DB field mutated, specific confirmation actually returned by the API rather than asserted in prose). The judge/verifier must satisfy each predicate against the trace/state; grounding every claim in an independently checkable source and enforcing a peer-review cycle. Admit only if all mandatory predicates pass.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding the judge with execution evidence by construction; (3) makes 'fabricate a confirmation number' fail a predicate ('confirmation must appear in a tool RESPONSE, not the agent's message').
methods.evidence: Rubric-based grounded verification with a peer-review cycle drives hallucination rates below 3% with 4-27% task gains reported across benchmarks; decomposition is the standard way to make LLM judging checkable rather than vibes-based.
methods.limitation: Requires per-domain rubric authoring (some human effort up front); an incomplete rubric leaves loopholes; still an LLM checking predicates unless the predicates are executed programmatically against state.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free and strongly portable because tau2/AppWorld expose the tool schema and state — many predicates can be checked PROGRAMMATICALLY against the env (no LLM), which fully escapes the foolable-judge bottleneck for those checks. Best default: programmatic predicates where possible, LLM-checked rubric for the rest.

admission.takeaway: No purely-textual gate escapes our bottleneck: any admission rule that ultimately reads only the transcript inherits the same <0.65-AUROC blind spot, and ensemble/jury/debate variants are provably insufficient because a cheat that is transcript-plausible (e.g., a fabricated confirmation number) fools correlated LLM judges in unison. The mechanisms that actually reduce reward-hacking of the gate all do ONE of two things: (a) replace the judge's input with the environment's own outcome — held-out real task success (L2-04), counterfactual with-vs-without reward deltas (L2-05), execution/state verification (L2-01), dual-control independent verification (L2-02, 15x fewer false successes), or programmatic rubric predicates checked against state (L2-15); or (b) statistically bound a noisy signal so noise-level 'improvements' cannot be admitted — pessimistic OPE lower bounds (L2-10), high-confidence/safe-policy-improvement guarantees (L2-11), conformal selective admission with abstention (L2-08), and FDR-controlled batch admission (L2-09) for the many-candidates multiple-comparisons problem. For tau2-bench and AppWorld specifically — both expose a deterministic verifiable reward and a queryable state — the highest-leverage, fully training-free pipeline is: seed entries only from env-verified successes (L2-01/02) -> accept via held-out validation success and counterfactual A/B (L2-04/05), made affordable by importance-weighted reuse of logged rollouts (L2-12) -> wrap the accept decision in a pessimistic LCB / conformal-FDR gate to control variance and multiple comparisons (L2-09/10) -> use a cheap LLM jury (L2-06) only as a first-stage triage whose DISAGREEMENT routes candidates to a grounded check or a small human-review budget via learning-to-defer (L2-13). Ensemble/debate methods are useful only as cost-savers and disagreement detectors layered on top of an outcome-grounded gate, never as the grounded gate itself. The one method that beats the judge on our exact benchmarks out-of-the-box is the learned trajectory detector (L2-03: AUROC 0.83 tau2 / 0.95 AppWorld, 4-8x more catches than the best judge), but it needs env-auto-labeled data and ~50% precision, so it belongs as triage, not autonomous admitter.

methods.name: Execute-Distill-Verify (EDV)
methods.one_liner: Parallel heterogeneous agents run the task, a third-party agent distills candidate experiences by comparing real trajectories, and a cross-model consensus vote gates admission with a default-reject policy.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2606.24428. Three stages: EXECUTE — 2 randomly-sampled models from a pool (MiMo, GLM-4.7, MiniMax-M2.1) run the same task in parallel producing diverse real trajectories; DISTILL — a separate third-party agent does comparative analysis across those trajectories to write candidate experiences (executors never self-summarize their own work, breaking the self-confirmation loop); VERIFY — the execution group consensus-votes each candidate: unanimous->shared memory, partial->private memory, rejected->discarded (default-reject). Purely offline experience construction over API models; no weight training.
methods.evidence: tau2-Bench: 86.6% pass@1 vs ReasoningBank 81.9% and Router 83.5%. Also Mind2Web and MMTB (58.10 vs 55.96) gains. Human audit of memory: groundedness 3.72->4.41 (5pt), noise 1.21->0.63. Contamination probe: injecting 10% erroneous-but-coherent memories into ReasoningBank dropped it 82.5%->77.2%, whereas EDV's voting blocks such entries at write time.
methods.portable_to_us: Directly portable and the closest turnkey match to our setting: API-only, multi-model pool, tau2 is a headline benchmark. A small group can implement it as-is (need 2-3 API model endpoints). Main cost is running each task through multiple models.
methods.limitation: 'Verify' is cross-model consensus voting, NOT a ground-truth DB/tool-state check — grounding comes only from comparing real trajectories, so if all voting models share the same blind spot (e.g. all rate a fabricated-confirmation trajectory as success) a cheat entry still passes. Weaker than true execution-state verification.
methods.addresses: (2) admission mechanism not relying on a single foolable judge (distributes the judge across models + default-reject) and partially (1) since candidates are distilled from executed trajectories rather than self-report.

methods.name: Cross-model / Council-mode consensus as admission gate
methods.one_liner: Route the admit/reject decision to several heterogeneous frontier models and require agreement, exploiting that self-consistent errors are model-specific and rarely overlap.
methods.mechanism: Council Mode (arXiv:2604.02923) dispatches the same (entry, evidence) to multiple heterogeneous models and majority-votes. Grounded in 'Too Consistent to Detect' (arXiv:2505.17656): a single model's confident errors are systematic and self-consistent, so it cannot catch them by re-reading its own output — but those errors seldom coincide across different LLMs, so a disagreeing peer flags them. Training-free, prompt-level.
methods.evidence: Fine-grained cross-model consistency checking improved hallucination-detection F1 by 6-39% over SOTA single-model methods. 2505.17656 shows self-consistent errors are model-specific and rarely overlap across LLMs, motivating an external verifier.
methods.portable_to_us: Cheap and immediately usable: replace our single-judge admission with 2-3 API models voting; keep an entry only on agreement. Complementary to EDV's voting stage. Feasible for a small group.
methods.limitation: Models trained on overlapping data can share correlated blind spots (a fabrication pattern all judges rate as success survives). Adds per-entry API cost. Does not add any non-transcript signal — still reads only the conversation.
methods.addresses: (2) admission mechanism not relying on a single foolable judge.

methods.name: Bayesian per-entry trust with reuse-driven decay (SuperLocalMemory)
methods.one_liner: Every memory entry carries a trust score updated Bayesianly from downstream task outcomes; low-trust entries are down-weighted in retrieval and auto-evicted before they compound.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2603.02240. Each memory starts with a prior trust value and is updated as evidence accrues from how its use correlates with agent success/failure; consistency validation flags entries contradicting reliable ones; outlier detection catches statistically anomalous memories; low-trust entries are evicted automatically. Preventive (removes influence before cascading errors), training-free, runs on runtime signals only.
methods.evidence: >85% task success maintained under 20-30% memory contamination; low-trust entries evicted within 3-5 interactions; <5% compute overhead; trust-weighted retrieval clearly beats reliability-agnostic baselines.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable as a wrapper on any experience library: attach a trust field, update it whenever an entry is retrieved and the resulting run is (verifiably) successful or not, decay+evict on repeated failure. Cheap.
methods.limitation: Chicken-and-egg with our exact bottleneck: the Bayesian update needs a trustworthy outcome signal, which is the thing we lack. Works only if paired with a real success check (execution state); if driven by the same foolable judge it will happily reinforce a cheat entry.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing bad entries from being retained at the source (eviction) and (2) reducing single-judge dependence — but only if fed a reliable outcome signal.

methods.name: Retrieval-stage poisoning filters: perplexity + consistency + LLM-verify
methods.one_liner: Training-free filters at retrieval that drop degraded entries by perplexity, contradiction with reliable context, and auxiliary-LLM coherence checks.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2601.05504 (Memory Poisoning Attack and Defense). Three stackable defenses applied before an entry influences the agent: (a) perplexity-based filtering removes linguistically anomalous entries; (b) consistency verification against current context flags entries that contradict established patterns; (c) an auxiliary LLM verifies coherence/relevance. All applied at retrieval, no retraining.
methods.evidence: Poisoning attack success rates drop substantially when defenses are active; combinations show cumulative protection while preserving legitimate-memory utility (paper reports recovery toward baseline performance).
methods.portable_to_us: Trivially portable and near-zero cost as a pre-retrieval filter in tau2/AppWorld pipelines.
methods.limitation: Directly weak against OUR failure mode: our harmful entries (e.g. 'fabricate a confirmation number and claim success') are fluent, low-perplexity, and internally consistent — perplexity and coherence filters will pass them. Useful against clumsy injected poison, not against plausible-but-fake self-generated experience.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing bad entries at the source, but only for low-quality/anomalous poison, not fluent fabrication.

methods.name: Governed memory with write-validation gate + reconciliation retro-audit (SSGM)
methods.one_liner: A governance middleware intercepts every memory write with a logical-contradiction gate against protected core facts, keeps an immutable ledger, and periodically reconciles/rolls back drifted memory against raw traces.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2603.11768. Write-Validation Gate uses a Truth Maintenance System rejecting updates where the new entry logically contradicts protected core facts. Weibull temporal-decay prunes stale entries below a freshness threshold. Dual-track storage: mutable active graph + immutable episodic ledger. An asynchronous Reconciliation operator periodically re-aligns drifted memory against raw interaction traces and offers rollback if behavioral degradation is detected. Cryptographic provenance marks trusted vs adversarial sources. Training-free architectural framework.
methods.evidence: Purely theoretical — NO empirical results. States testable hypotheses (governance gates bound asymptotic drift to O(N*eps) vs naive O(T*eps); access-scoped retrieval reduces leakage). References LongMemEval/MemoryBench but runs no experiments.
methods.portable_to_us: The reconciliation/retro-audit + immutable-ledger + rollback ideas are portable and valuable: periodically re-check stored experiences against the raw execution logs that produced them and roll back entries that no longer verify. Requires building the ledger + a re-check routine.
methods.limitation: No evidence it works; the TMS gate needs formalizable 'core facts' (hard for open tool-use); provenance/audit infra is unspecified. Design guidance, not a validated method.
methods.addresses: (3) prevention/retention hygiene at source and (1)/(2) via retro-audit that re-grounds entries against raw traces.

methods.name: Provenance-tracked / evidence-tracing memory
methods.one_liner: Every experience entry carries provenance fields (who wrote it, source, trust-at-write, and a derived_from chain to the exact tool outputs/observations) so a retro-audit can check the claim was actually grounded in execution.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2606.04990 (survey) + provenance-field designs: each record stores created_by, source_protocol, trust_score, provenance_chain; each entity/relation/instruction carries a source object (kind, reference span, location, derived_from). Attribution links a stored claim/action back to the evidence units (tool outputs, env observations) that produced it; Audit reconstructs executions for inspection. Reconciliation weights entries by provenance and calibration.
methods.evidence: Survey/framework-level; establishes that provenance supports attribution, audit, and accountability as process-level trust functions. No single success-rate number (it is a design + survey layer), but underpins concrete systems above.
methods.portable_to_us: Highly portable and cheap: at write time, force each experience to cite the specific tool calls / DB writes that justify its success claim; at audit time, reject or quarantine entries whose cited evidence does not show the goal state was reached. This is a direct route to grounding admission in execution artifacts for tau2/AppWorld.
methods.limitation: Provenance only helps if you actually verify the cited evidence — recording a provenance chain that points at a transcript still fails if nobody checks the DB end-state. It supplies the plumbing, not the verification decision itself.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding the judge with execution evidence (ties each entry to concrete tool-call artifacts) and (3) source-side prevention via retro-audit.

methods.name: Decentralized dual-pool memory (DecentMem)
methods.one_liner: Split memory into a verified exploitation pool and a candidate exploration pool, and keep it decentralized per-role so behaviors do not homogenize and collapse to one dominant (possibly cheat) strategy.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2605.22721. Each agent maintains its own dual-pool memory: an exploitation pool of consolidated/validated past trajectories and an exploration pool of LLM-generated candidates for unseen contexts. Decentralization is deliberate — a shared central pool makes every agent retrieve the same items, homogenizing behavior until the system collapses to a single dominant strategy, destroying the diversity that motivated multiple agents.
methods.evidence: Shows centralized memory undermines role-complementary specialization and drives collapse toward a single strategy; decentralized dual-pool preserves diversity (paper reports improved multi-agent task performance vs centralized memory).
methods.portable_to_us: Portable single-agent adaptation: keep a two-tier library — a candidate pool (new experiences) and a promoted pool (only entries that passed verification) — and only retrieve for high-stakes use from the promoted pool. Prevents unverified/cheat candidates from dominating retrieval.
methods.limitation: Framed for multi-agent role diversity; single-agent benefit is mostly the exploitation/exploration separation rather than role diversity. Does not itself verify entries — promotion still needs a verification gate.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing bad entries from taking over the library (mode-collapse to a cheat strategy) and staging admission via a candidate->promoted gate.

methods.name: Entropy monitoring + reinjection to prevent collapse
methods.one_liner: Track the diversity/entropy of the experience library and reinject exploration when it decays, since without intervention entropy falls monotonically into a degenerate single-behavior collapse.
methods.mechanism: Entropy Collapse framework + Entropy Reinjection Protocol (ERP). Innovation decline erodes diversity; entropy collapse is substrate-independent (holds for RL action spaces and NL communication alike). ERP monitors entropy and reinjects diversity so entropy stops decaying monotonically into collapse.
methods.evidence: Framework shows entropy decays monotonically into collapse without ERP across substrates; ERP arrests the decay. Evidence is mechanistic/simulation-level rather than agent-benchmark success rates.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable as a monitor: measure the behavioral/strategy diversity of retrieved experiences (e.g. clustering of tool-use patterns); when it collapses, force retrieval of diverse or exploratory entries. Cheap to instrument.
methods.limitation: Abstract/heuristic — thresholds and the entropy metric for a text experience library are undefined; reinjecting diversity can add noise if not paired with quality/verification. Prevents monoculture, not fabrication per se.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing degenerate/collapsed libraries (guards against a single cheat strategy crowding out diverse valid behavior).

methods.name: Quality-Diversity archive for the skill/experience library (MAP-Elites / CycleQD)
methods.one_liner: Bin experiences by behavioral descriptor and keep the best entry per bin, so the library illuminates the whole strategy space instead of collapsing to the single highest-scoring (possibly gamed) entry.
methods.mechanism: MAP-Elites keeps an archive of high-performing AND behaviorally-diverse solutions across a discretized feature space, 'illuminating' rather than converging to one optimum; CycleQD (ICLR 2025) applies cyclic MAP-Elites to LLM agent skill acquisition; QD has also been used to keep diverse prompt/skill archives. Applied to an experience library: define behavioral descriptors (task type, tool set, strategy), keep best-per-bin by a fitness = verified success.
methods.evidence: QD/MAP-Elites is a mature, evidenced paradigm for maintaining diverse high-quality archives (CycleQD demonstrates agent skill acquisition; QD variants maintain diverse prompt and attack archives). Evidence is on diversity/coverage and downstream skill quality rather than tau2 specifically.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable: replace 'keep top-k by score' retention with 'keep best-per-behavioral-bin', which structurally prevents one dominant fabrication 'skill' from monopolizing retrieval and preserves coverage of legitimate strategies. Moderate design effort to choose descriptors.
methods.limitation: QD optimizes for diversity, not correctness — it only helps if the fitness function is a VERIFIED success signal; with a foolable judge as fitness it will faithfully preserve diverse cheat strategies. Descriptor design is manual.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing collapse/cheat-strategy dominance at the source; must be combined with a real verifier as fitness to also address (1).

methods.name: Capability-Preserving Evolution / regression-gating updates
methods.one_liner: Guard each library update against destructive drift by regression-testing the evolved library on a held-out capability probe before committing, since self-evolution is non-monotonic and can silently degrade prior skills.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2605.09315 ('Do Self-Evolving Agents Forget?'). Documents that self-evolution is non-monotonic — adapting to new tasks progressively degrades previously-acquired capabilities across workflow, skill, model, and memory evolution. Capability-Preserving Evolution (CPE) constrains destructive capability drift during continual adaptation (only commit updates that do not regress a preserved capability set).
methods.evidence: Empirically demonstrates catastrophic capability degradation during continual adaptation across four evolution axes; CPE mitigates the drift (paper reports preserved performance on prior tasks vs unconstrained evolution).
methods.portable_to_us: Portable guardrail: maintain a small held-out suite of already-solved tau2/AppWorld tasks; before admitting a new batch of experiences, re-run the suite and reject the update if it regresses. Catches a bad/cheat entry that helps one task but breaks others.
methods.limitation: Requires a maintained probe/regression set and re-running it (compute cost); catches regressions but not a cheat entry that improves the metric via the same fabrication the judge can't see. Targets forgetting more than fabrication.
methods.addresses: (3) preventing harmful updates from being retained (regression gate) and (2) provides a second, task-level admission signal beyond the judge.

methods.name: Hindsight experience relabeling grounded in achieved state (AgentHER)
methods.one_liner: Instead of storing 'this failed' confabulations, read what the trajectory ACTUALLY achieved from real tool interactions and relabel it as a correct (goal, trajectory) pair.
methods.mechanism: arXiv:2603.21357. Lifts Hindsight Experience Replay to LLM agents: determine what was genuinely accomplished across unstructured multi-step tool interactions (from the real interaction outcomes, not the agent's claim), then synthesize the natural-language goal that the trajectory actually satisfies, storing a grounded positive example. Safe negative-experience learning: failures become correct data instead of self-reinforcing-error rules.
methods.evidence: Demonstrates converting failed trajectories into useful relabeled experiences that improve agent learning; grounding is in actual achieved state rather than transcript self-report.
methods.portable_to_us: Strong fit where end-state is readable (tau2 DB state, AppWorld API effects): relabel every trajectory by the actual DB/tool end-state, which INJECTS the non-transcript signal our judge lacks and structurally prevents 'fabricated success' from being stored as success. Feasible training-free.
methods.limitation: Requires the ability to read achieved state — the exact signal we're missing in the general case. It converts our problem into 'instrument the environment for state readout', which is the right move but non-trivial for some tasks.
methods.addresses: (1) grounding admission in execution evidence (relabel by real achieved state) and (3) safe negative-experience learning without confabulation.

methods.name: Contrastive good-vs-bad experience distillation
methods.one_liner: Store paired successful and failed trajectories and distill only the divergence point — the exact step where reasoning went wrong — so entries teach the correct fix and inoculate against the failure/cheat pattern.
methods.mechanism: DistiLLM-2 (arXiv:2502; ICML 2025) contrastively raises likelihood of good answers while lowering the student's own mistakes; 'Learning from Failure' (arXiv:2402.11651) integrates negative examples as implicit contrastive signal; Experiential Reflective Learning (arXiv:2603.24639) and Self-Consolidation's contrastive extraction isolate the high-information difference between correct and incorrect solutions rather than storing raw trajectories. Training-free variant: prompt an LLM to extract the contrastive insight from a verified success/failure pair (ExpeL-style, but with verified labels).
methods.evidence: DistiLLM-2 shows contrastive good/bad distillation beats identical-loss baselines; negative-example integration improves agent tuning; contrastive extraction yields compact high-value insights.
methods.portable_to_us: Training-free contrastive insight extraction is directly portable to a text experience library: for each task keep a verified-good and a verified-bad trajectory and store the distilled divergence. The bad side can explicitly encode 'do NOT fabricate confirmation IDs' as an anti-pattern.
methods.limitation: DistiLLM-2 itself is weight-training. The training-free variant still needs CORRECT labels for which trajectory succeeded — with a foolable judge, a mislabeled pair teaches the wrong lesson, and reflective-rule libraries (ExpeL) are known to propagate a single confabulated rule across all environments. Correctness of the contrast depends on upstream verification.
methods.addresses: (3) source-side prevention (entries actively teach against the cheat pattern), contingent on (1)/(2) supplying correct success/failure labels.

methods.name: Anti-reward-hacking verifier hardening: task-agnostic soft-checks + transition credit
methods.one_liner: Harden the admission gate against being gamed by adding cheap task-agnostic sanity checks and by scoring correctness transitions rather than final-answer success.
methods.mechanism: Reward Hacking Benchmark (arXiv:2605.02964) shows agents exploit shortcuts (skipping verification, inferring from task-adjacent metadata, tampering with eval functions) and can overwhelm LLM judges by embedding complex code in tool calls — but a task-agnostic 'style' soft-check on the verifier neutralizes that exploit. ReCrit (arXiv:2605.18799) moves the unit of credit from final-answer success to correctness-transition, making sycophancy vs genuine correction separable optimization targets. Training-free as prompt/heuristic layer.
methods.evidence: RHB: exploit rates 0% (Claude Sonnet 4.5) to 13.9% (DeepSeek-R1-Zero); adding the style soft-check effectively closed the judge-overwhelm exploit. ReCrit shows separating transition-credit distinguishes sycophancy from capability.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable: add cheap task-agnostic rules to the admission gate that flag experiences instructing the agent to fabricate IDs, skip verification steps, or tamper with the checker, and measure the library's exploit rate on an RHB-style probe. Low cost.
methods.limitation: Heuristic soft-checks give incomplete adversarial coverage — a novel cheat pattern not in the ruleset slips through; still fundamentally a smarter judge, not execution grounding.
methods.addresses: (2) hardening the admission judge so it is harder to fool, and (3) explicitly screening cheat/reward-hack entries at the source.

methods.name: Confabulation guardrails: require causal execution evidence before writing a rule
methods.one_liner: Detect and quarantine reflective-memory confabulations by requiring each stored rule to cite the specific tool-call evidence that causally supports it, rejecting rules generalized from a single unverifiable trajectory.
methods.mechanism: 'Honest Lying: Memory Confabulation in Reflexive Agents' (arXiv:2605.29463) plus the ExpeL vulnerability analysis: reflective memory suffers self-reinforcing error — an agent that falsely concludes an approach 'always fails' never retests it, and binary feedback without causal diagnosis is the triggering condition; a confabulated rule then applies across every environment. Guardrail: before admitting a rule, require it to cite the concrete tool-call / state-change evidence that causally justifies it, and quarantine any rule that overgeneralizes from one trajectory or lacks execution support.
methods.evidence: Papers characterize the confabulation/self-reinforcing-error failure mode and its trigger (binary feedback preventing causal diagnosis); establishes that confabulated rules generalize destructively. Mechanistic/diagnostic evidence rather than a headline success-rate.
methods.portable_to_us: Portable design rule for our library: reject any experience/rule not backed by a citation to the execution log showing the goal state was reached; quarantine single-trajectory generalizations until corroborated by a second verified run. Cheap to enforce at write time.
methods.limitation: Mostly a design principle; enforcement still depends on having execution logs to cite (the missing signal). Guards against overgeneralization and unsupported rules but is not a full verifier on its own.
methods.addresses: (2) admission not relying on a foolable judge (requires causal evidence, not plausibility) and (3) preventing self-reinforcing confabulated/cheat rules at the source.

source.takeaway: The load-bearing lesson across these 14 methods is that our bottleneck cannot be fixed by a better transcript-reading judge — almost every training-free text-only filter (perplexity, LLM-verify, contrastive extraction, single-model self-check, even Bayesian trust and QD) fails against fluent fabrication or silently reinforces it, because their signal is still the same transcript the judge already sees. Two levers actually change the game. Lever A — INJECT A NON-TRANSCRIPT SIGNAL: ground admission in execution artifacts, via provenance/evidence-tracing that ties each entry to the specific tool calls / DB writes that justify its success claim (2606.04990), hindsight relabeling by the ACTUAL achieved end-state so 'fabricated success' can never be stored as success (AgentHER 2603.21357), and periodic reconciliation/retro-audit that re-checks stored entries against raw logs and rolls back ones that no longer verify (SSGM 2603.11768).
